title: How to use a 'letter of credit' for a large international project
primary_keyword: letter of credit for international project

author.name: Samuel Chen
author.bio: A former product manager at a major fintech company, Samuel has deep expertise in the global payments landscape. He analyzes financial tools and strategies to help freelancers maximize their earnings and minimize fees.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Mandate a Letter of Credit for any new international contract valued over $100,000 to eliminate catastrophic payment risk.
To make a service contract bankable, define payment milestones with objective, verifiable criteria, such as a 'client-signed acceptance form,' rather than subjective goals.
When using a Letter of Credit, budget for a 2-6 week setup delay and fees of 1-2% of the contract value, ensuring the project does not start until the LC is confirmed.

Select your payment security tool based on the primary risk: use a Letter of Credit for ultimate security on enterprise deals, and a Merchant of Record (MoR) to offload global tax and compliance burdens on other high-value projects.
summary: Professionals face the significant problem of ensuring payment for high-value international service contracts. The article's core advice is to use a Letter of Credit (LC) for catastrophic-risk projects by treating it as a standard professional tool with verifiable milestones, while opting for more agile solutions like Digital Escrow or a Merchant of Record for less critical engagements. By strategically selecting the appropriate payment security tool for the specific risk, readers can eliminate financial uncertainty and pursue global opportunities with confidence.
ai_overview_answer: A Letter of Credit (LC) secures payment for high-value service contracts by acting as a binding, irrevocable financial promise from your client's bank. To obtain one, you must frame it as a standard professional procedure and prepare key documents, including a Master Service Agreement and a Scope of Work with objective, verifiable milestones. For single projects, an Irrevocable Letter of Credit (ILOC) is used, while a Standby Letter of Credit (SBLC) serves as a backup payment method for ongoing engagements. Unlike with physical goods, your proof of performance is a document, such as a client-signed acceptance form for a specific milestone. This tool is most critical for international contracts over $100,000 where non-payment would pose a catastrophic risk to your business. The process typically takes two to six weeks and involves bank fees ranging from 0.75% to 2% of the total contract value. For smaller or faster-moving projects, alternatives like digital escrow or Merchant of Record platforms are often more practical.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

@graph.name: How to Secure a Letter of Credit

step.name: Frame the Request as a Mark of Professionalism.
step.text: When negotiating a high-value contract, position the LC not as a sign of distrust, but as your standard operating procedure. You might say, "For a project of this scope, my standard practice is to secure the payment structure through a letter of credit. It's a mechanism that provides complete transparency and payment security for both of us." This language reframes you as a serious business entity with established processes, instantly elevating your stature.

step.name: Prepare Your Documentation (The Service-Based Edition).
step.text: Your intellectual property requires impeccably clear documentation. Prepare these three key documents: A Master Service Agreement (MSA) defining the overall legal relationship. A detailed Scope of Work (SOW) outlining the project, timelines, and responsibilities. A Schedule of Milestones with objective, verifiable acceptance criteria. A bank cannot verify "strategic objectives" but it can verify a "client-signed acceptance form for Milestone A." Your payment triggers must be binary—completed or not—and evidenced by a document.

step.name: Approach Your Business Bank with a Clear Ask.
step.text: Once your contract is ready, contact your business banker. State your needs clearly: "I am the beneficiary of an upcoming international contract for [$Amount]. I require our bank to act as the advising and confirming bank for an irrevocable letter of credit that will be issued by the client's bank." Arriving with your MSA and SOW in hand demonstrates your preparation and will dramatically accelerate the process.

step.name: Factor in the Real Costs and Timelines.
step.text: A letter of credit is a powerful risk management tool, but it is neither free nor instantaneous. Costs: Banks charge fees for this service, which can range from 0.75% to over 2% of the total contract value. For a $200,000 project, this means budgeting $1,500 to $4,000 in fees that may be split between you and the client. Timelines: The negotiation and issuance process is not overnight. From the initial request to the final LC being in place, you should budget two to six weeks. This timeline is critical—the project should not kick off until the LC is fully executed and confirmed by your bank.

body.content: The Letter of Credit, Reimagined: Securing High-Value Service Contracts

The initial rush of landing a major international project is often followed by a critical question: "How do I ensure I get paid?" This is the moment you must shift from artist to architect, structuring the deal to protect your work and your revenue. The traditional tool for this, the Letter of Credit (LC), is not just for those moving physical goods. For the elite global professional, it is a powerful instrument for securing payment for services rendered, and mastering its mechanics is a mark of true business acumen.

Think of an LC less like a shipping document and more like a binding financial promise from your client's bank to you. Instead of a "bill of lading," your "proof of performance" might be an approved strategy document, the successful deployment of a block of code, or a client sign-off on a key project milestone. This framework transforms your intellectual property into a bankable asset.
To navigate this landscape, you must first understand the key players:

items: **The Applicant:** Your client, who applies to their bank to open the LC in your favor.
**The Beneficiary:** You—the expert who will be paid under the terms of the LC.
**The Issuing Bank:** The client's bank, which makes the irrevocable promise to pay.
**The Advising/Confirming Bank:** Typically your bank, which verifies the LC’s authenticity and, if it's "confirmed," adds its own guarantee of payment.

body.content: For high-value service engagements, you only need to focus on two primary types of LCs. For a one-off project, the **Irrevocable Letter of Credit (ILOC)** is your fortress; once issued, it cannot be changed or canceled without your consent. For longer-term engagements, the **Standby Letter of Credit (SBLC)** is your ally. It acts as a backup payment method: you invoice as normal, but if the client defaults, you can draw on the SBLC.
How to Secure a Letter of Credit: A Four-Step Process

Knowing this tool exists is one thing; operationalizing it is another. This isn't a complex mystery reserved for multinational corporations. It's a straightforward process you can manage with confidence by shifting your mindset from "asking for a favor" to "directing a standard financial procedure."

items:
**Step 1: Frame the Request as a Mark of Professionalism.**
When negotiating a high-value contract, position the LC not as a sign of distrust, but as your standard operating procedure. You might say, *"For a project of this scope, my standard practice is to secure the payment structure through a letter of credit. It's a mechanism that provides complete transparency and payment security for both of us."* This language reframes you as a serious business entity with established processes, instantly elevating your stature.

**Step 2: Prepare Your Documentation (The Service-Based Edition).**
Your intellectual property requires impeccably clear documentation. Prepare these three key documents:
*   A **Master Service Agreement (MSA)** defining the overall legal relationship.
*   A detailed **Scope of Work (SOW)** outlining the project, timelines, and responsibilities.
*   A **Schedule of Milestones** with objective, verifiable acceptance criteria. A bank cannot verify "strategic objectives" but it *can* verify a "client-signed acceptance form for Milestone A." Your payment triggers must be binary—completed or not—and evidenced by a document.

**Step 3: Approach Your Business Bank with a Clear Ask.**
Once your contract is ready, contact your business banker. State your needs clearly: *"I am the beneficiary of an upcoming international contract for [$Amount]. I require our bank to act as the advising and confirming bank for an irrevocable letter of credit that will be issued by the client's bank."* Arriving with your MSA and SOW in hand demonstrates your preparation and will dramatically accelerate the process.

**Step 4: Factor in the Real Costs and Timelines.**
A letter of credit is a powerful risk management tool, but it is neither free nor instantaneous.
*   **Costs:** Banks charge fees for this service, which can range from 0.75% to over 2% of the total contract value. For a $200,000 project, this means budgeting $1,500 to $4,000 in fees that may be split between you and the client.
*   **Timelines:** The negotiation and issuance process is not overnight. From the initial request to the final LC being in place, you should budget two to six weeks. This timeline is critical—the project should not kick off until the LC is fully executed and confirmed by your bank.

body.content: When Does an LC Make Sense (And When Is It Overkill)?

Understanding the mechanics is only half the battle; true strategic leadership is knowing precisely when to deploy this instrument. It's about matching the level of risk management to the level of actual risk, ensuring you remain both protected and agile.

items:
**GREEN LIGHT: Use an LC When the Stakes are Catastrophic.**
This is non-negotiable territory. For any international project valued over $100,000, consider an LC standard procedure. A payment default on a contract of this magnitude isn't just a setback; it could be a business-ending event. This is especially true with a new client or one based in a country with a less stable legal system. The LC removes catastrophic payment risk from the equation.

**YELLOW LIGHT: Consider an LC for Mid-Range Projects with New Clients.**
In the $25,000 to $100,000 range, the decision becomes more nuanced. A careful cost-benefit analysis is essential. If you're entering a complex project with a new client, an LC can provide invaluable peace of mind. As Eric Baron, a Principal at the banking law firm Aldrich & Bonnefin, PLC, explains, a Standby LC is particularly effective here. It acts as a safety net, triggered only if the client defaults, providing robust security without altering your standard invoicing flow.

**RED LIGHT: Avoid LCs for Smaller, Faster-Moving Projects.**
For contracts under $25,000 or with long-term, trusted clients, an LC is almost always strategic overkill. The slow, bureaucratic nature undermines the speed and flexibility that make you valuable. The fees would represent a disproportionately large percentage of the contract, and the setup time would create unnecessary friction.

rows: Client Relationship
Establishes professionalism and secure terms from day one.
Can introduce a layer of formality that feels rigid.
Project Complexity
Guarantees payment upon hitting objective milestones.
Requires extremely precise, bank-verifiable milestone definitions.
Financial Cost
Eliminates the risk of non-payment on a significant contract.
Fees can consume 1-2% of the project's total value.
Time & Admin
Forces clear documentation and terms upfront.
The setup process can delay the project start by several weeks.

body.content: Beyond the Bank: Faster, Smarter Alternatives

While the traditional LC provides an unparalleled guarantee for catastrophic-risk deals, its rigid nature makes it impractical for the majority of projects. For a modern Business-of-One that thrives on agility, faster and more digitally native solutions are required.

items:
**Option 1: Digital Escrow Services.**
A service like Escrow.com acts as a trusted neutral third party. Your client pays the project fee into a secure account at the start; the service releases the funds to you upon delivery and approval. This is significantly faster and more affordable than an LC and solves the problem of non-payment. However, it does *not* solve the underlying operational burdens of international trade, such as calculating and remitting consumption taxes (like VAT or GST) in your client's country—a complex liability that remains entirely on your shoulders.

**Option 2: Merchant of Record (MoR) Platforms.**
This is the definitive evolution for the professional who wants comprehensive protection. A Merchant of Record (like Gruv) acts as a reseller of your services. Your client contracts with and pays the MoR; the MoR then pays you. This model provides the payment security of an escrow service but adds a critical layer: the MoR assumes all financial and legal liability for the transaction. This includes managing global tax compliance, collecting and remitting VAT/GST, and handling chargebacks, freeing you to focus exclusively on your work.

body.content: The CEO's Choice: A Framework for Control
Your decision ultimately comes down to a single question: **What specific risk are you trying to eliminate?**

rows: Ultimate Security on Enterprise Deals
Letter of Credit
Provides an irrevocable, bank-backed guarantee of payment for high-stakes, catastrophic-risk projects.
Extremely slow, expensive, and complex. Creates significant administrative overhead that hinders agility.
A Balance of Speed & Payment Security
Digital Escrow
A fast, affordable way to ensure funds are secured before work begins, eliminating non-payment risk.
Offers zero protection from the complexities of global tax, legal, and regulatory compliance. The liability remains yours.
Security, Speed, & Total Compliance
Merchant of Record
Guarantees payment while also legally removing the entire burden of international tax and compliance from your business.
The MoR is the legal seller of record, which means their name, not yours, appears on the final payment receipt.

body.content: Take Control: Your Playbook for Fearless International Growth

Securing payment is not an administrative chore; it's the ultimate act of a CEO, a strategic decision that protects the value of your intellectual property. The Letter of Credit is an unparalleled tool for de-risking massive, company-defining contracts. But for most engagements, the landscape of international trade has evolved beyond the legacy bank-to-bank guarantee.
True control comes from knowing precisely which tool to deploy. To guarantee payment from an international client, think of your options as a hierarchy of control, moving from least to most complex:

items: **100% Upfront Payment:** The gold standard for smaller projects or clients with immense trust.
**Milestone Payments via a Merchant of Record:** Secures funds while legally insulating you from global tax and compliance liability.
**Digital Escrow Service:** A neutral third party holds the full project fee, eliminating non-payment risk but leaving compliance burdens on you.
**Letter of Credit:** The ultimate guarantee, reserved for enterprise-level contracts where a payment default would be a critical blow to your business.

body.content: This framework clarifies your strategic options, allowing you to move from a place of reasonable anxiety to a position of strategic command.

rows: Best For
Six-figure+ "bet the company" projects with new enterprise clients or in high-risk jurisdictions.
High-value client work where speed, efficiency, and compliance simplicity are top priorities.
Core Benefit
An irrevocable, bank-backed payment guarantee that is virtually impossible to default on.
Seamless payment security combined with the complete offloading of global sales tax and compliance liability.
Primary Risk Managed
**Catastrophic Non-Payment Risk:** Protects against a client defaulting on a career-defining sum of money.
**Operational & Compliance Risk:** Protects against the immense complexity of international invoicing, currency conversion, and consumption tax remittance.
Speed & Agility
Slow. The process is bureaucratic and can take 2-6 weeks, requiring significant lead time.
Fast. Onboarding is typically quick, allowing you to start projects and get paid with minimal administrative friction.
Compliance Burden
Low. The bank handles the payment transfer, but you are still liable for your own tax and business compliance.
Zero. The MoR becomes the legal reseller of your service, assuming full liability for calculating, collecting, and remitting taxes like VAT/GST.

body.content: Choosing the right path is the final step in mastering your financial risk management. Armed with this knowledge, you are no longer just a service provider hoping to get paid. You are the strategic and empowered CEO of your Business-of-One, ready to pursue ambitious global projects, secure in the knowledge that your expertise will be rewarded, your risks are managed, and your growth is truly fearless.
